phase.name: Network Simplex Min-Cost Flow Solver
phase.exit_criteria: Public Go module github.com/branched-services/go-mcf builds; Layer 1-3 tests green; go test -race clean; gofmt/go vet clean; >=85% statement coverage; LICENSE/NOTICE/SPDX headers present.

tasks.description: Scaffold the public Go module and create all legal/attribution artifacts. Create go.mod at module path github.com/branched-services/go-mcf targeting Go 1.22 with github.com/holiman/uint256 as the sole runtime dependency. Create LICENSE with the Boost Software License 1.0 text verbatim. Create NOTICE with LEMON attribution (Egervary Research Group on Combinatorial Optimization, 2003-2018, distributed under BSL-1.0). Create README.md whose first paragraph states that this library is DeFi-oriented (*uint256.Int flow/capacity, int64 cost) and that general MCF users with int64 flow should use a different library; follow with a minimal working Solve code example and a closing section linking to LICENSE/NOTICE. Create doc.go containing only the package-level doc comment and the SPDX header so go doc has an anchor.
tasks.commit: chore(scaffold): add module, license, notice, and README
steps: Step 1: Create go.mod at module path github.com/branched-services/go-mcf with 'go 1.22' directive and require github.com/holiman/uint256 at the current latest stable minor release; run go mod tidy to populate go.sum
Step 2: Create LICENSE containing the full Boost Software License 1.0 text verbatim (no modifications, no added headers)
Step 3: Create NOTICE naming LEMON (https://lemon.cs.elte.hu), copyright 2003-2018 Egervary Research Group on Combinatorial Optimization, distributed under the Boost Software License 1.0

Step 4: Create README.md -- first paragraph declares DeFi/uint256 scope (uint256 flow/capacity, int64 cost) and directs general int64-flow users elsewhere; include a minimal Solve example; last section links to LICENSE and NOTICE
Step 5: Create doc.go with the // SPDX-License-Identifier: BSL-1.0 header on line 1 and a package-level doc comment summarizing the public API
acceptance_criteria: go.mod declares module github.com/branched-services/go-mcf and go 1.22
go mod tidy runs clean and go.sum is committed
LICENSE contains Boost Software License 1.0 text verbatim
NOTICE contains LEMON attribution
README.md first paragraph declares DeFi/uint256 scope; contains a minimal Solve code block; ends with LICENSE/NOTICE links
doc.go line 1 is exactly '// SPDX-License-Identifier: BSL-1.0'
tasks.implementation_notes: Scaffolded go.mod (go 1.22, uint256 v1.3.2), LICENSE (BSL-1.0), NOTICE (LEMON attribution), README.md (DeFi scope + Solve example + license links), doc.go (SPDX header + package doc). Commit c679a7e.

tasks.description: Declare the public API surface in mcf.go: type Arc{From, To int; Cost int64; Capacity, Flow *uint256.Int}; type Result{TotalFlow *uint256.Int; TotalCost int64}; function Solve(ctx context.Context, arcs []Arc, n, source, sink int, demand *uint256.Int) (Result, error); sentinels ErrInfeasible and ErrInvalidInput. Solve is initially a stub that returns (Result{}, errors.New("mcf: not implemented")) -- downstream tasks replace the body. Godoc on Result.TotalCost notes the value is advisory and may under-report for very large flows. Godoc on Solve states the concurrency contract: safe across goroutines on independent inputs; callers must not share the same []Arc slice across concurrent Solve calls because Arc.Flow is written in place. No other symbols are exported from the package root.
tasks.commit: feat(api): declare Arc, Result, Solve, and error sentinels
steps: Step 1: Create mcf.go with SPDX header, package declaration, and imports (context, errors, github.com/holiman/uint256)
Step 2: Define type Arc with fields From int, To int, Cost int64, Capacity *uint256.Int, Flow *uint256.Int; add godoc explaining Flow is written in place on success
Step 3: Define type Result with fields TotalFlow *uint256.Int and TotalCost int64; godoc on TotalCost documents the best-effort truncation window from FR-10
Step 4: Declare sentinels ErrInfeasible (errors.New("mcf: demand cannot be routed from source to sink")) and ErrInvalidInput (errors.New("mcf: invalid input"))

Step 5: Declare Solve with the exact signature from the spec; initial body returns (Result{}, errors.New("mcf: not implemented")); godoc states the concurrency contract and that ctx cancellation returns (Result{}, ctx.Err()) with no partial results
acceptance_criteria: go build ./... succeeds
go doc github.com/branched-services/go-mcf lists exactly Arc, Result, Solve, ErrInfeasible, ErrInvalidInput -- no other exported symbols from the package root
errors.Is(ErrInfeasible, ErrInfeasible) is true; errors.Is(ErrInvalidInput, ErrInvalidInput) is true; the two sentinels are distinct
Solve godoc states concurrency contract (no shared []Arc across concurrent calls) and context cancellation behavior
mcf.go line 1 is exactly '// SPDX-License-Identifier: BSL-1.0'
tasks.implementation_notes: Declared Arc, Result, Solve, ErrInfeasible, ErrInvalidInput in mcf.go with SPDX header. Solve stub returns not-implemented error. Godoc documents concurrency contract and TotalCost advisory window. Commit b9581d3.

tasks.description: Implement the FR-3 precondition validation at Solve entry, runs before any pivot work. Validate: n >= 2; 0 <= source, sink < n; source != sink; demand non-nil and non-zero; every arc's From and To in [0, n); no self-loops (From != To); every Capacity non-nil; every arc satisfies |Cost| * (n + 1) < MaxInt64 / 8 (overflow guard). First violation returns (Result{}, err) where errors.Is(err, ErrInvalidInput) is true and the wrapped message names the exact failed condition (e.g., "mcf: invalid input: arcs[5].From (7) out of range [0,4)"). Validation is a package-internal function called from the Solve stub before it returns not-implemented. Include a table-driven test that exercises every individual precondition failure and asserts errors.Is(err, ErrInvalidInput).
tasks.commit: feat(validate): add Solve input precondition validation
steps: Step 1: Add an unexported validate(arcs []Arc, n, source, sink int, demand *uint256.Int) error function that checks preconditions in the order listed in docs/spec.md Numerical Preconditions and FR-3
Step 2: For each violation, return fmt.Errorf("mcf: invalid input: <specific message>: %w", ErrInvalidInput) so errors.Is(err, ErrInvalidInput) is true and the message identifies the failure
Step 3: Compute the cost overflow bound as |arc.Cost| * int64(n+1) using int64 arithmetic with abs handled carefully (avoid overflow on math.MinInt64); compare against MaxInt64 / 8
Step 4: Wire validate into Solve -- call it before the not-implemented stub; on validate error return (Result{}, err)

Step 5: Write validate_test.go with table-driven cases for every precondition: n<2, source out of range, sink out of range, source==sink, demand nil, demand zero, arc From/To out of range, self-loop, nil Capacity, cost overflow boundary (both interior-pass and boundary-fail); each case asserts errors.Is(err, ErrInvalidInput) and asserts the wrapped message contains the identifying keyword
acceptance_criteria: Every precondition in FR-3 / Numerical Preconditions has at least one failing-case unit test
errors.Is(err, ErrInvalidInput) holds for every precondition failure
Wrapped error messages name the specific condition (indices, values) so a caller can diagnose without additional logging
Cost overflow check correctly guards |cost| * (n+1) < MaxInt64 / 8 without itself overflowing on math.MinInt64
Valid inputs pass validate without error (validate returns nil)
go vet ./... and gofmt -d . clean
tasks.implementation_notes: Implemented validate() with all FR-3 precondition checks; wired into Solve; table-driven tests cover every failure case plus valid input. Commit 3f1f1eb

tasks.description: Implement the solver state struct and Big-M initialization that builds the artificial spanning tree before the pivot loop. Solver state holds tree-layout slices (parent, pred_arc, thread, rev_thread, succ_num, last_succ, direction, state), int64 node potentials pi, the arc list (real arcs + artificial arcs appended), preallocated *uint256.Int scratch buffers for bottleneck computation and flow updates, and the Big-M constant M = MaxInt64 / (8 * (n + 1)). Initialization adds an artificial root node (index n) plus one artificial arc per real node connecting that node to root. Artificial arc for source node carries +demand (source->root), sink node carries -demand (root->sink), other nodes carry 0; all at cost M. Artificial arcs form the initial spanning tree (parent[i]=n, pred_arc[i]=artificial-arc-index, direction set accordingly). Real arcs start as non-tree at STATE_LOWER with Flow zeroed. Initial potentials pi[root]=0; pi[i] = +/-M so that reduced cost of each artificial arc is zero. Artificial arcs are never exposed through the public API. Provide an unexported accessor that the test layer can call (without reflection) to inspect the tree/state after init.
tasks.commit: feat(solver): add solver state and Big-M artificial tree init
steps: Step 1: Create solver.go with SPDX header; define state flag constants stateLower=0, stateTree=-1, stateUpper=1 (matching LEMON's sign convention); define directionDown=-1, directionUp=1

Step 2: Define unexported type solver struct with fields: n int, source, sink int, demand *uint256.Int, arcs []Arc (callers' slice, captured), artArcs []Arc (artificial arcs owned by solver), pi []int64, parent, predArc, thread, revThread, succNum, lastSucc, direction, state []int, M int64, plus preallocated *uint256.Int scratch buffers (bottleneck, tmpFlow) for the pivot hot path

Step 3: Implement newSolver(arcs []Arc, n, source, sink int, demand *uint256.Int) *solver -- allocate slices with length n+1 (real nodes + artificial root), compute M = MaxInt64 / (8 * int64(n+1)), preallocate scratch *uint256.Int values, and zero real-arc Flow pointers (s.Set(uint256.NewInt(0)))

Step 4: Implement initializeTree() -- for each real node i in [0, n): append an artificial Arc with cost M linking i to the root (n); set tree-layout arrays (parent[i]=n, predArc[i]=artificial-arc-index, direction by supply sign, thread/revThread linking root down through the real nodes in order, succNum and lastSucc accordingly). Source gets +demand supply (arc direction source->root, carries demand), sink gets -demand (root->sink, carries demand), other nodes get 0 (pre-set to cost M but zero flow)

Step 5: Set initial potentials pi such that every artificial arc has reduced cost zero (pi[root]=0; pi of a node with supply +demand has pi=-M or +M per arc direction chosen); set every real arc's state to stateLower

Step 6: Add an unexported snapshot() function (or equivalent exported-only-for-test accessor via a _test.go helper in same package) that returns the post-init tree structure the tests can assert against without reflection

Step 7: Write init_test.go -- post-init asserts: parent/thread/revThread form a valid spanning tree of n+1 nodes; source artificial arc carries demand, sink artificial arc carries demand, other artificial arcs carry 0; every real arc has state=stateLower and Flow.IsZero(); every artificial arc's reduced cost (cost - pi[from] + pi[to], using LEMON's convention) equals zero
acceptance_criteria: After newSolver + initializeTree, tree-layout arrays represent a valid spanning tree on n+1 nodes rooted at n (each non-root has exactly one parent; thread traversal visits every node exactly once)
Big-M equals MaxInt64 / (8 * (n + 1))
Source's artificial arc carries flow equal to demand; sink's artificial arc carries flow equal to demand; all other artificial arcs carry zero flow
Every real arc starts with state=stateLower and Flow.IsZero() true
Every artificial arc's reduced cost is zero immediately after init
Scratch *uint256.Int buffers on the solver struct are non-nil and reused across pivots (no per-pivot allocation)
go vet and gofmt clean; race-free
tasks.implementation_notes: Implemented solver struct with tree-layout slices, Big-M constant, scratch buffers, and artificial spanning tree initialization. Commit 36a3705.

tasks.description: Implement block-search pricing: partition non-tree arcs into blocks of ~sqrt(E), scan one block per call rotating the starting index, and within the scanned block select the arc with the largest state-dependent violation. Violation expression per docs/spec.md: (state == stateLower) ? -reducedCost : (state == stateUpper) ? +reducedCost : 0. An arc is eligible only when violation > 0. Reduced cost formula matches LEMON: rc = cost - pi[from] + pi[to] (int64 arithmetic, cannot overflow given the validation bound). Skip arcs whose endpoint potentials are sentinel 'unreachable' values. Return the entering arc index (or sentinel -1 if no block in a full rotation contains an eligible arc, which signals optimality). Rotating starting index is seeded deterministically from problem size (e.g., block_size = ceil(sqrt(len(arcs+artArcs))); next_block = 0 initially, advances each call modulo numBlocks) -- no RNG, preserving FR-level determinism.
tasks.commit: feat(pricing): add block-search pricing with state-dependent violation
steps: Step 1: Add unexported reducedCost(s *solver, arcIdx int) int64 returning cost - pi[from] + pi[to] for either a real arc or an artificial arc (unified indexing space)
Step 2: Add field blockSize int and nextBlock int on the solver struct; initialize blockSize = max(1, int(math.Ceil(math.Sqrt(float64(totalArcs))))) and nextBlock = 0 in newSolver

Step 3: Implement selectEntering() int that scans up to numBlocks blocks starting at nextBlock (wrapping), each block covering [b*blockSize, min((b+1)*blockSize, totalArcs)); within the first block containing any eligible arc, pick the arc with max violation and return it; advance nextBlock past the scanned block
Step 4: Compute violation: if state==stateLower violation = -rc; if state==stateUpper violation = +rc; else 0. Treat arcs in the tree (state==stateTree) as ineligible
Step 5: Sentinel-potential skip -- if pi[from] or pi[to] equals a sentinel (near MaxInt64 or MinInt64, defined as a package-level constant), skip the arc (do not consider it eligible)
Step 6: Return -1 when no block in a full rotation contains any eligible arc; this is the optimality signal for the Solve loop

Step 7: Write pricing_test.go -- constructed solver state with known reduced costs; assert selectEntering returns the largest-violation arc in the first block that contains one; assert -1 when nothing is eligible; assert rotation by calling selectEntering repeatedly and observing the starting index advances
acceptance_criteria: reducedCost returns cost - pi[from] + pi[to] and matches hand-computed values in unit tests
selectEntering returns the arc index with the largest positive violation in the first block scanned that contains any eligible arc
selectEntering returns -1 after a full block rotation produces no eligible arc (optimality signal)
Rotating starting index advances deterministically with no RNG
blockSize equals ceil(sqrt(totalArcs)), clamped to >= 1
Arcs with sentinel (unreachable) potentials are skipped
go vet and gofmt clean
tasks.implementation_notes: Implemented reducedCost helper and selectEntering with block-search pricing. Block size is ceil(sqrt(totalArcs)), rotation is deterministic. Sentinel potentials are skipped. Commit 91323e1.

tasks.description: Implement join-node (LCA) discovery and leaving-arc selection with the strongly-feasible-tree tie-break. Given an entering arc with endpoints (u, v), walk up from both using parent pointers and use succNum to equalize depth before walking in lockstep until the two pointers meet -- that meeting node is the join (cycle apex). Leaving-arc selection walks the cycle from both sides of the entering arc to the join, computing bottleneck residual capacity using preallocated uint256 scratch buffers (no allocation in the hot path). The bottleneck for a forward arc in the cycle is capacity - flow; for a backward arc it is flow. Among candidates tied at the minimum bottleneck, pick the arc closest to the join node on the side opposite the entering arc's orientation -- this is the strongly-feasible-tree tie-break, the real anti-cycling mechanism (Bland's rule is insufficient). A code comment in the tie-break block must state this explicitly so future readers do not delete it as redundant. Return (joinNode, leavingArcIdx, bottleneckFirstSide, bottleneckSecondSide) -- the caller performs the actual push in GM-0007.
tasks.commit: feat(pivot): add LCA join and leaving-arc selection with SFT tie-break
steps: Step 1: Implement findJoin(enterFrom, enterTo int) int -- use succNum to know subtree sizes and walk up from the deeper endpoint until depths match, then advance both in lockstep until parent pointers converge; return the shared ancestor index

Step 2: Implement findLeaving(enterArc int, join int) (leavingArc int, firstBottleneck, secondBottleneck *uint256.Int) -- walk cycle first side (from enterFrom to join, treating arc orientation correctly) and second side (enterTo to join), tracking min residual; use preallocated scratch uint256 to compute residual without allocating

Step 3: Among tied-minimum candidates, implement the SFT tie-break: pick the arc closest to join on the side opposite the entering arc's orientation. Add a comment block inside the tie-break code explaining: 'This tie-break maintains the strongly-feasible-tree invariant. Do not remove: Bland's rule alone cannot prevent cycling in degenerate pivots.'
Step 4: Result bottlenecks are returned via the solver's scratch buffers, not via newly-allocated *uint256.Int; caller consumes them in GM-0007's push step

Step 5: Write join_test.go + leaving_test.go -- constructed trees with known LCA, assert findJoin returns correct apex; constructed cycles with known bottlenecks, assert findLeaving returns the minimum-residual arc AND that tied candidates are resolved by SFT tie-break (construct a case where Bland's-rule pick differs from SFT pick)
acceptance_criteria: findJoin returns the correct LCA for all tested tree configurations including symmetric and asymmetric subtree depths
findLeaving selects the minimum-residual arc in the cycle

SFT tie-break resolves tied candidates consistently and picks the arc closest to join on the side opposite the entering arc's orientation -- verified in a dedicated unit test where the SFT pick differs from a naive first-seen pick
Tie-break code contains a prominent comment explaining the strongly-feasible-tree invariant and forbidding deletion
Zero allocations in the hot path -- verified via testing.AllocsPerRun on a tight loop that reuses the solver
go vet and gofmt clean
tasks.implementation_notes: Implemented findJoin (LCA via succNum depth proxy) and findLeaving (bottleneck residual with SFT tie-break on second side). Added deltaFirst, deltaSecond, scratch buffers to solver. Zero-allocation hot path verified. Commit 745a78c.

tasks.description: Implement pivot execution: push bottleneck flow around the cycle using in-place uint256 arithmetic, swap the entering arc into the tree and the leaving arc out, update node potentials for the affected subtree only, and handle degenerate pivots by flipping arc state without modifying the tree. Flow push uses Arc.Flow.Add and Arc.Flow.Sub -- never + or - operators, never intermediate allocations; forward arcs in the cycle receive bottleneck, backward arcs lose it. When the entering arc's direction through the cycle coincides with the leaving arc's orientation such that bottleneck equals zero (degenerate pivot), flip the entering arc's state (stateLower <-> stateUpper) and leave the tree untouched -- the strongly-feasible-tree invariant (maintained by GM-0006's tie-break) guarantees this cannot cycle. Subtree potential update: delta = reducedCost(entering) (sign per LEMON's convention based on cycle direction); walk the subtree rooted at the endpoint that detaches from the old tree via thread pointers until lastSucc and add delta to pi for each visited node -- int64 arithmetic, bounded to subtree size. Update state flags: entering arc becomes stateTree; leaving arc becomes stateLower if bottleneck direction matches or stateUpper otherwise.
tasks.commit: feat(pivot): execute in-place flow push, tree swap, and subtree potential update
steps: Step 1: Implement pushFlow(enterArc, leaveArc, joinNode int, bottleneck *uint256.Int) -- walk the cycle from both sides of the entering arc to join, for each arc apply Arc.Flow.Add(bottleneck) on forward-direction arcs and Arc.Flow.Sub(bottleneck) on backward-direction arcs; all arithmetic in place on caller-owned Flow pointers
Step 2: Degenerate-pivot branch -- if bottleneck.IsZero() and leavingArc == enteringArc, flip state[enteringArc] between stateLower and stateUpper and return without touching the tree

Step 3: Implement updateTree(enterArc, leaveArc, joinNode int) -- rewire parent, predArc, thread, revThread, succNum, lastSucc, direction arrays to reflect the basis swap; set state[enterArc]=stateTree and state[leaveArc] to whichever bound the leaving arc settled at

Step 4: Implement updatePotentials(enterArc, subtreeRoot int) -- compute delta = reducedCost(enterArc) with sign per LEMON cycle direction; walk thread pointers starting at subtreeRoot until lastSucc adding delta to pi[node]; O(subtree size), int64 only
Step 5: Compose these into pivot(enterArc, leaveArc, joinNode int, bottleneck *uint256.Int) called from the main loop (actual wiring lives in GM-0008)

Step 6: Write pivot_test.go -- hand-constructed cycles with known bottlenecks; after pivot assert flow values on every cycle arc changed by exactly bottleneck (forward +, backward -), tree arrays match expected shape, pi of the detached subtree shifted by expected delta, and entering/leaving state flags match expected values; include a degenerate-pivot test (bottleneck == 0) asserting state flip and unchanged tree
acceptance_criteria: Post-pivot Arc.Flow values on cycle arcs match hand-computed expected values exactly
Forward-arc flows increase by bottleneck; backward-arc flows decrease by bottleneck; no other arc's Flow changes
Tree-layout arrays (parent, predArc, thread, revThread, succNum, lastSucc, direction) are valid after the swap -- every node still has exactly one path to root
pi values of the detached subtree shift by the exact expected delta; other pi values are unchanged
Degenerate-pivot (bottleneck==0, leaving==entering) flips state[enteringArc] between stateLower and stateUpper and leaves all tree arrays and flows unchanged
testing.AllocsPerRun on a pivot-reuse loop reports zero allocations in the pivot hot path
go vet and gofmt clean
tasks.implementation_notes: Implemented pushFlow, updateTree (with rebuildDFS for thread/succNum/lastSucc reconstruction), updatePotentials, and pivot with degenerate-pivot handling. Zero-alloc hot path via pre-allocated childHead/childNext scratch arrays. Commit 17c80b2.

tasks.description: Wire the end-to-end Solve entry point: validation (GM-0003) -> solver init (GM-0004) -> pivot loop (selectEntering GM-0005, findJoin+findLeaving GM-0006, pivot GM-0007) -> feasibility check on artificial arcs -> Result build. Pivot loop checks ctx.Err() at loop-iteration boundaries; on cancellation returns (Result{}, ctx.Err()) with no partial result. Termination condition: selectEntering returns -1 (no block contains an eligible arc). Feasibility check: if any artificial arc carries nonzero flow at optimality, return (Result{}, ErrInfeasible). On success, construct Result with TotalFlow = demand (post-condition: TotalFlow.Eq(demand) must hold -- assert and return an internal error if not), and accumulate best-effort TotalCost per FR-10: for each pivot, if bottleneck.IsUint64() and int64(bottleneck.Uint64()) * arcCost does not overflow int64, add to TotalCost; otherwise skip for that pivot. Remove the not-implemented stub from GM-0002 once fully wired. No iteration cap.
tasks.commit: feat(solve): wire Solve entry point, feasibility check, and cancellation
steps: Step 1: Replace the not-implemented stub in mcf.go's Solve with the real body: call validate; on error return (Result{}, err)
Step 2: Construct the solver via newSolver; call initializeTree

Step 3: Main loop -- for { if err := ctx.Err(); err != nil { return (Result{}, err) }; enter := selectEntering(); if enter == -1 { break }; join := findJoin(from, to); leaveArc, bottleneck := findLeaving(enter, join); update best-effort TotalCost per FR-10 before mutating flow; pivot(enter, leaveArc, join, bottleneck) }
Step 4: Post-loop feasibility check -- iterate artificial arcs; if any has Flow.Sign() != 0, return (Result{}, ErrInfeasible)

Step 5: Build Result -- set TotalFlow to a freshly-allocated *uint256.Int copy of demand; assert TotalFlow.Eq(demand) as a post-condition (paranoia guard; if violated return an internal error that does not match ErrInfeasible or ErrInvalidInput)

Step 6: Write mcf_test.go -- at least two end-to-end tests: a simple feasible instance with hand-computed optimum (asserts returned Result.TotalFlow.Eq(demand), per-arc Flow values match); a simple infeasible instance (demand > max-flow capacity, asserts errors.Is(err, ErrInfeasible)); a cancelled-context test (start a long-running solve in a goroutine, cancel the context, assert (Result{}, ctx.Err()) and errors.Is(err, context.Canceled))
acceptance_criteria: Feasible end-to-end instance returns (Result{TotalFlow, TotalCost}, nil) with Result.TotalFlow.Eq(demand) true and per-arc Flow values matching hand-computed optimum
Infeasible end-to-end instance returns (Result{}, err) with errors.Is(err, ErrInfeasible) true
Cancelled-context test returns (Result{}, ctx.Err()) with errors.Is(err, context.Canceled) true and the returned Result is the zero value
Best-effort TotalCost: accumulates when bottleneck.IsUint64() and product fits int64; skips otherwise (verified in a dedicated unit test using large uint256 bottleneck)
No iteration cap in the loop; termination comes from selectEntering returning -1
go vet ./... and gofmt -d . clean; go test -race ./... passes
tasks.implementation_notes: Wired Solve entry point with pivot loop, feasibility check, cancellation, and best-effort TotalCost. Fixed flow direction bugs in findLeaving/pushFlow and pivot leaveArc==enterArc handling. Commit 73f6bdd.

tasks.description: Implement the reusable checkSolution test helper (Layer 2) called after every Layer 1 and Layer 3 test case. Given the post-solve arcs, n, source, sink, demand, and Result, checkSolution asserts every correctness invariant from FR-7 and fails the test with a message that names the specific violated invariant when any one fails. Invariants: (1) flow conservation at every node other than source and sink -- sum of incoming Flow equals sum of outgoing Flow; (2) capacity feasibility -- 0 <= Flow <= Capacity per arc; (3) optimality certificate -- non-tree arcs at stateLower have reduced cost >= 0, at stateUpper have reduced cost <= 0 (reconstruct reduced cost from Arc.Cost and post-solve potentials exposed via an unexported solver-state accessor); (4) demand satisfied -- TotalFlow.Eq(demand); (5) cost consistency -- sum(arc.Cost * arc.Flow) equals Result.TotalCost within the best-effort truncation window (when all bottlenecks fit IsUint64 the equality is exact; otherwise the helper only asserts TotalCost is not obviously wrong). Helper lives in checksolution_test.go (test-only file). Include a self-test that constructs a known-good solution and asserts checkSolution passes, and a deliberately-broken solution that asserts checkSolution fails with the expected named invariant.
tasks.commit: test(invariants): add checkSolution Layer 2 invariant helper
steps: Step 1: Create checksolution_test.go with SPDX header and package clause (same package so it can reach unexported types)
Step 2: Implement checkSolution(t *testing.T, arcs []Arc, n, source, sink int, demand *uint256.Int, result Result) -- t.Helper() at the top

Step 3: For each invariant, t.Errorf with a message starting 'checkSolution: flow conservation violated at node X: in=Y, out=Z' / 'checkSolution: capacity bound violated at arc i: flow=X, capacity=Y' / 'checkSolution: optimality certificate violated at arc i: state=S, reduced_cost=R' / 'checkSolution: TotalFlow != demand: TotalFlow=X, demand=Y' / 'checkSolution: TotalCost inconsistent: expected=X, actual=Y'
Step 4: To access post-solve potentials for the reduced-cost check, expose an unexported debug snapshot accessor (only compiled via a _test.go build helper in the same package -- not a public API)

Step 5: Add a checksolution_helper_test.go with two cases -- a hand-constructed valid solution asserting checkSolution produces no failures, and a hand-constructed invalid solution asserting checkSolution produces a failure message containing the expected invariant keyword
acceptance_criteria: checkSolution exercises all five invariants from FR-7
Each invariant violation produces a distinct, named failure message identifying the violated invariant and the offending index
Self-test with a hand-constructed valid solution passes checkSolution with no failures
Self-test with each of five deliberately-broken solutions (one per invariant) produces a failure naming the expected invariant
checkSolution is reachable from all Layer 1 and Layer 3 test files (same package)
go vet and gofmt clean
tasks.implementation_notes: Implemented checkSolution with 5 FR-7 invariants (flow conservation, capacity feasibility, optimality certificate, demand satisfied, cost consistency). Extracted unexported solve() from Solve() for test access to solver snapshot. Self-tests cover valid solution and all 5 violation types via fakeT. Commit c1c0c0d.

tasks.description: Port Layer 1 correctness cases from LEMON's test/min_cost_flow_test.cc -- small hand-constructed graphs with known optimal cost and flow, feasibility/infeasibility detection, zero-supply / zero-demand edges, parallel arcs, and negative-cost arcs. Each ported case lives in mcf_test.go (or layer1_test.go), invokes Solve, and calls checkSolution from GM-0009 to verify invariants. Expected optimal costs are taken directly from LEMON's test file (which is distributed under BSL-1.0 so the numeric ground truth can be copied; only algorithmic structure must be rewritten). Translate LEMON's int-flow test inputs into uint256 by setting capacities via uint256.NewInt. Where LEMON's test uses int supply/demand at multiple nodes, collapse to a single source->sink demand by inserting an internal routing if necessary; document the translation in a comment above the affected test so the correspondence to the LEMON case is traceable.
tasks.commit: test(layer1): port LEMON min_cost_flow_test correctness cases
steps: Step 1: Identify LEMON cases in test/min_cost_flow_test.cc that map to single-source single-sink demand semantics; for each one record: graph edge list with costs and capacities, source, sink, demand, expected optimal cost, expected feasibility

Step 2: For each selected case add a Go subtest t.Run(name, func(t *testing.T) { ... }) that builds []Arc, calls Solve, asserts expected error (nil or errors.Is ErrInfeasible), asserts Result.TotalCost equals expected within FR-10's best-effort window, and calls checkSolution
Step 3: Include at minimum the following ported cases: one trivial single-arc feasible, one parallel-arc case, one negative-cost arc, one infeasibility case, one zero-capacity arc (inert)
Step 4: Add comments at the top of each case citing the LEMON source test line or identifier so reviewers can cross-reference
Step 5: Verify all tests pass and checkSolution produces no failures
acceptance_criteria: At least 5 Layer 1 cases ported from LEMON covering the feasibility/infeasibility/parallel-arc/negative-cost/zero-capacity categories listed in docs/spec.md Testing Strategy
Every ported case calls checkSolution and passes all five invariants
Every ported case asserts Result.TotalCost equals the LEMON-provided expected value within FR-10's truncation window
Every test case carries a source-citation comment linking it to the LEMON test file
go test -race ./... passes
tasks.implementation_notes: Ported 7 Layer 1 test cases from LEMON test/min_cost_flow_test.cc: trivial single-arc, parallel arcs, negative-cost arc, infeasibility, zero-capacity inert arc, LEMON main graph (sup2, 12 nodes, 21 arcs, expected cost 7620), and negative-cost path routing. All cases call checkSolution and verify TotalCost against LEMON expected values. Commit 95e1dd8.

tasks.description: Implement Layer 3 structural pattern tests plus the SPDX-header lint and coverage gate. Structural tests per docs/spec.md Testing Strategy Layer 3: (a) negative-cost cycle entirely within the input graph -- solver finds correct optimum with no special handling; (b) parallel arcs with strictly increasing costs -- lower-cost arc saturates before higher-cost parallel arc; (c) sparse multi-hop graph -- many intermediate nodes, few direct arcs; (d) single-unit demand (demand=1) routes along min-cost path; (e) demand equals graph max-flow capacity -- saturates every path; (f) demand = max-flow + 1 returns ErrInfeasible; (g) large uint256 capacity exceeding MaxUint64 -- flow correctness holds and TotalCost falls into the FR-10 skip-accumulation path (verified by constructing a test that forces that branch). Each case calls checkSolution. Add TestSPDXHeaders that walks every *.go file under the module root and asserts line 1 is exactly '// SPDX-License-Identifier: BSL-1.0'. Finally, verify coverage: the task is complete only when go test -race -cover ./... reports >= 85% statement coverage across non-test files.
tasks.commit: test(layer3): add structural patterns, SPDX lint, and coverage gate
steps: Step 1: Add one test per structural pattern a-g; each builds []Arc, calls Solve, asserts expected error class, and calls checkSolution

Step 2: Case (g) -- large uint256 capacity -- must use a capacity value whose Uint64 representation would overflow; demand must be chosen such that at least one pivot's bottleneck has IsUint64()==false, forcing TotalCost skip-accumulation; the test asserts TotalFlow.Eq(demand) and tolerates a reduced TotalCost

Step 3: Case (a) -- negative-cost cycle -- construct a graph with a cycle whose total cost is negative and arcs outside the cycle feeding source/sink; the optimum must use the cycle; assert checkSolution passes and TotalCost matches hand-computed expected
Step 4: Implement TestSPDXHeaders walking *.go under module root (including _test.go files per FR-11) and asserting line 1 matches exactly '// SPDX-License-Identifier: BSL-1.0'
Step 5: Run go test -race -cover ./... and verify statement coverage is >= 85% on non-test files; if below, add targeted tests until the threshold is met (or document an accepted gap with reason)
Step 6: Run gofmt -d . and go vet ./... and confirm both are clean
acceptance_criteria: All seven structural tests (a-g) pass
Case (a) -- negative-cost cycle -- finds the correct optimum without any solver-level special casing (no graph mutation, no cycle-breaking shim)
Case (b) -- parallel arcs with increasing costs -- post-solve the lower-cost parallel arc is saturated before any flow appears on the higher-cost parallel arc
Case (f) -- demand = max-flow + 1 -- returns errors.Is(err, ErrInfeasible)
Case (g) -- large uint256 capacity -- TotalFlow.Eq(demand) holds and TotalCost accumulation is partially skipped per FR-10 (verified by inspecting Result.TotalCost against a hand-bound range)
TestSPDXHeaders passes with zero violations across every *.go file in the module
go test -race -cover ./... reports >= 85% statement coverage on non-test files
gofmt -d . produces no diff; go vet ./... is clean
tasks.implementation_notes: Implemented all 7 structural tests (a-g), TestSPDXHeaders, verified 97.3% coverage and race-clean. Commit 710e4f4.
